content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 26

**Chapter:** Leviticus 26 (46 verses) — Covenant Blessings and Curses
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Date arbitrated:** 2026-03-06
**Sources:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Summary

Total disagreements logged: **62**

Leviticus 26 is the most terminologically contentious chapter in the Holiness Code because of its unique legal vocabulary — especially *qeri* (appearing seven times), the four occurrences of the sevenfold formula, and the repeated *ratzah* / *ga'al* / *ma'as* roots that create deliberate verbal echoes across the chapter. PNEUMA consistently paraphrases the chapter's legal-covenantal terminology, requiring the most arbiter overrides. The chapter's key theological verse (v. 12) and the grace-floor verse (v. 44) each generate multiple disagreements over rendering the chapter's most significant Hebrew.

---

## Disagreements by Verse

### Verse 1

**D-001 | V. 1 | "For yourselves" placement**
- EMET: "You shall not make for yourselves elilim"
- LOGOS: "You shall not make for yourselves idols, nor shall you erect a carved image or a pillar for yourselves"
- PNEUMA: "Do not make idols for yourselves"
- Ruling: LOGOS (formal structure; "for yourselves" present in both prohibitions as in MT)

**D-002 | V. 1 | Transliteration of elilim**
- EMET: "elilim [idols]" (preserves Hebrew with gloss)
- LOGOS: "idols"
- PNEUMA: "idols"
- Ruling: "idols" — per project convention, English rendering in consensus text

**D-003 | V. 1 | "Sacred stone" vs. "pillar" for massebah**
- EMET: "pillar [matsevah]"
- LOGOS: "pillar"
- PNEUMA: "sacred stone"
- Ruling: "pillar" — per project convention established in Genesis series and maintained through Leviticus

**D-004 | V. 1 | "Figured stone" vs. "sculpted stone" for even maskiyit**
- EMET: "figured stone"
- LOGOS: "figured stone"
- PNEUMA: "sculpted stone"
- Ruling: "figured stone" — ESV/NASB standard; EMET/LOGOS agree

**D-005 | V. 1 | Sentence structure (unified vs. fragmented)**
- EMET: single unified conditional sentence
- LOGOS: single unified conditional sentence
- PNEUMA: breaks into three separate imperative sentences
- Ruling: unified prohibition sentence (EMET/LOGOS) — the MT's syntax is a single prohibition sequence; PNEUMA's fragmentation loses this unity

### Verse 2

**D-006 | V. 2 | "Reverence" vs. "stand in awe of" for tira'u**
- EMET: "fear my sanctuary" / "reverence my sanctuary"
- LOGOS: "reverence my sanctuary"
- PNEUMA: "stand in awe of my sanctuary"
- Ruling: "reverence my sanctuary" — LOGOS; formal register appropriate for legal covenant text

### Verse 3

**D-007 | V. 3 | "Observe" vs. "keep" for shemartem**
- EMET: "keep my commandments"
- LOGOS: "observe my commandments"
- PNEUMA: "keep my commandments"
- Ruling: "observe my commandments" — LOGOS; "observe" is slightly more formal and fits the covenant-legal register

**D-008 | V. 3 | "Do them" vs. "carry them out"**
- EMET: "do them"
- LOGOS: "do them"
- PNEUMA: "carry them out"
- Ruling: "do them" — EMET/LOGOS; *asitem* is the plain *asah* ("to do/make"); "carry them out" (PNEUMA) adds processual nuance not in the Hebrew

### Verse 4

**D-009 | V. 4 | "Season" vs. "time" for be'itam**
- EMET: "in their time"
- LOGOS: "in their season"
- PNEUMA: "at the right time"
- Ruling: "in their season" — LOGOS; "season" better reflects the agricultural calendar nuance of *be'it* (appointed time/season) than "time" (EMET) or "the right time" (PNEUMA)

**D-010 | V. 4 | Addition of "autumn rains and spring rains"**
- EMET: no addition
- LOGOS: no addition
- PNEUMA: adds "the autumn rains and the spring rains"
- Ruling: no addition — EMET/LOGOS; this interpretive expansion is not in the MT of v. 4

### Verse 5

**D-011 | V. 5 | "Eat your bread to the full" vs. "eat your fill of food"**
- EMET: "eat your bread to satisfaction"
- LOGOS: "eat your bread to the full"
- PNEUMA: "eat your fill of food"
- Ruling: "eat your bread to the full" — LOGOS; MT has *lechem* (bread/food specifically); "food" (PNEUMA) is vaguer; "to the full" is more idiomatic than "to satisfaction" (EMET)

**D-012 | V. 5 | "Live securely" vs. "dwell in your land securely"**
- EMET: "dwell in your land securely"
- LOGOS: "dwell in your land securely"
- PNEUMA: "live securely in your land"
- Ruling: "dwell in your land securely" — EMET/LOGOS; "dwell" for *yashavtem* is the more precise covenant-land verb than "live"; word order follows LOGOS

### Verse 6

**D-013 | V. 6 | "Harmful beasts" vs. "dangerous animals" vs. "evil beasts"**
- EMET: "evil beasts [chayah ra'ah]"
- LOGOS: "harmful beasts"
- PNEUMA: "dangerous animals"
- Ruling: "harmful beasts" — LOGOS; "harmful" for *ra'ah* in this context more natural in English than "evil" (EMET) or "dangerous" (PNEUMA, modern safety register)

**D-014 | V. 6 | "Pass through" vs. "go through"**
- EMET: "pass through your land"
- LOGOS: "go through your land"
- PNEUMA: "pass through your country"
- Ruling: "pass through your land" — EMET/PNEUMA; "pass through" is more established for *ta'avor* than LOGOS's "go through"; "land" over PNEUMA's "country"

**D-015 | V. 6 | "Give peace" vs. "bring peace"**
- EMET: "I will give peace [shalom]"
- LOGOS: "I will give peace"
- PNEUMA: "I will bring peace"
- Ruling: "I will give peace" — EMET/LOGOS; *natati* is "give," not "bring" (PNEUMA)

### Verse 7

**D-016 | V. 7 | "Chase" vs. "pursue" for radaf**
- EMET: "you shall chase your enemies"
- LOGOS: "You shall chase your enemies"
- PNEUMA: "You will pursue your enemies and cut them down before you"
- Ruling: "You shall chase your enemies" — EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "and cut them down" which is not in the MT of v. 7 (that language belongs with v. 7's "fall before you by the sword")

### Verse 8

**D-017 | V. 8 | "Chase" vs. "put to flight"**
- EMET: "five shall chase a hundred"
- LOGOS: "Five of you shall chase a hundred"
- PNEUMA: "Five of you will chase a hundred... will put ten thousand to flight"
- Ruling: "Five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand" — LOGOS; "chase" used consistently in both halves of the verse; PNEUMA uses "chase" for the first and "put to flight" for the second, breaking the verbal parallel that is present in the MT

### Verse 9

**D-018 | V. 9 | "Turn to you" vs. "turn toward you"**
- EMET: "I will face toward you [paniti aleikhem]"
- LOGOS: "I will turn to you"
- PNEUMA: "I will turn toward you"
- Ruling: "I will turn to you" — LOGOS; compact and accurate; "toward" (PNEUMA) is an acceptable variant but slightly weaker directionally

**D-019 | V. 9 | "Make you fruitful and multiply you"**
- EMET: "I will make you fruitful and I will multiply you"
- LOGOS: "make you fruitful and multiply you"
- PNEUMA: "I will make you fruitful and multiply you"
- Ruling: "make you fruitful and multiply you" — LOGOS (after "I will turn to you"); minor difference in "and I will" (EMET/PNEUMA) vs. simple coordinate (LOGOS); LOGOS reads more fluidly

### Verse 10

**D-020 | V. 10 | "Old grain long kept" vs. "last year's harvest"**
- EMET: "old, very old [yashan noshan] grain you shall eat"
- LOGOS: "You shall eat old grain long kept"
- PNEUMA: "You will still be eating last year's harvest"
- Ruling: "You shall eat old grain long kept" — LOGOS; *yashan noshan* (doubled adjective, "old, very old") is best captured by "old grain long kept" which preserves the doubled emphasis. PNEUMA's "last year's harvest" collapses the doubled superlative and underspecifies — the point is that the grain is more than a year old

**D-021 | V. 10 | "Clear out the old to make way for the new"**
- EMET: "you shall bring out the old [yashen] from before the new [chadash]"
- LOGOS: "you shall clear out the old to make way for the new"
- PNEUMA: "you have to clear it out to make room for the new"
- Ruling: "you shall clear out the old to make way for the new" — LOGOS; "make way for" is more formal than "make room for" (PNEUMA); LOGOS over EMET for idiom

### Verse 11

**D-022 | V. 11 | "Make my dwelling" vs. "give my dwelling-place"**
- EMET: "I will give my dwelling-place [mishkani] in your midst"
- LOGOS: "I will make my dwelling among you"
- PNEUMA: "I will make my dwelling among you"
- Ruling: "I will make my dwelling among you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; "make my dwelling" is more idiomatic for the tabernacle-presence than "give my dwelling-place" (EMET)

**D-023 | V. 11 | "Among you" vs. "in your midst"**
- EMET: "in your midst"
- LOGOS: "among you"
- PNEUMA: "among you"
- Ruling: "among you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; slightly more natural English while preserving the *be-tokheikhem* sense of interior presence

**D-024 | V. 11 | "My soul shall not abhor you" vs. "I will not turn away from you in disgust"**
- EMET: "my soul shall not abhor/reject you [lo-tigh'al nafshi etkhem]"
- LOGOS: "my soul shall not abhor you"
- PNEUMA: "I will not turn away from you in disgust"
- Ruling: "my soul shall not abhor you" — LOGOS/EMET; the *nefesh* ("soul") of God is explicitly present in the MT and must be preserved; the same construction recurs positively-negated in v. 30 ("my soul shall abhor you") — the verbal echo is theologically crucial; PNEUMA removes *nefesh* from the clause

### Verse 12

**D-025 | V. 12 | "Walk among you" vs. "walk about among you"**
- EMET: "I will walk about in your midst [ve-hithalakhti be-tokheikhem]"
- LOGOS: "I will walk among you"
- PNEUMA: "I will walk among you"
- Ruling: "I will walk among you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; while EMET's "walk about" captures the Hithpael iterative force, the consensus prefers the cleaner "walk among you" as the standard rendering of this canonical verse. Note: the Hithpael is recorded in the Council Notes

**D-026 | V. 12 | "In your midst" vs. "among you"**
- EMET: "in your midst"
- LOGOS: "among you"
- PNEUMA: "among you"
- Ruling: "among you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; at this apex verse, "among you" is more natural and more universally recognized in the verse's canonical form

### Verse 13

**D-027 | V. 13 | "From the house of slavery" — addition**
- EMET: no addition
- LOGOS: no addition
- PNEUMA: adds "from the house of slavery"
- Ruling: no addition — EMET/LOGOS follow MT of Lev 26:13; "house of slavery" is the Exod 20:2 / Deut 5:6 formula but does not appear in the MT of this verse

**D-028 | V. 13 | "Walk erect" vs. "walk with your heads held high"**
- EMET: "made you walk upright/erect [qomemiyut]"
- LOGOS: "made you walk erect"
- PNEUMA: "made you walk with your heads held high"
- Ruling: "made you walk erect" — EMET/LOGOS; *qomemiyut* (hapax, "upright stature") is best captured by "erect" — a single precise word; PNEUMA's paraphrase adds "heads" which is interpretive

**D-029 | V. 13 | "That you should not be their slaves" vs. "so that you would no longer be their slaves"**
- EMET: "that you should not be their slaves"
- LOGOS: "that you should not be their slaves"
- PNEUMA: "so that you would no longer be their slaves"
- Ruling: "that you should not be their slaves" — EMET/LOGOS; "no longer" (PNEUMA) introduces a temporal nuance not in the MT's *me-heyot lahem avadim* ("from being their slaves"); the construction is purpose/result, not temporal

### Verse 15

**D-030 | V. 15 | "Spurn" vs. "reject" for tima'asu**
- EMET: "reject my statutes [tima'asu et-chuqqotai]"
- LOGOS: "spurn my statutes"
- PNEUMA: "reject my statutes"
- Ruling: "spurn my statutes" — LOGOS; "spurn" carries a stronger contemptuous dismissal force matching *ma'as*; creates verbal echo with v. 44 ("I will not spurn them") — this cross-reference is theologically important

**D-031 | V. 15 | "Abhors my rules" vs. "turns away from my laws"**
- EMET: "your soul loathes my ordinances [tigh'al nafshekhem et-mishpatai]"
- LOGOS: "your soul abhors my rules"
- PNEUMA: "your soul turns away from my laws"
- Ruling: "your soul abhors my rules" — LOGOS; "abhors" for *ga'al* is precise; creates echo with "my soul shall not abhor you" (v. 11) and "my soul shall abhor you" (v. 30) — the same root *ga'al* runs through all three; PNEUMA's "turns away from" loses this verbal connection

### Verse 16

**D-032 | V. 16 | "Visit you with panic" vs. "bring sudden terror"**
- EMET: "I will appoint over you panic [behalah]" / "I will visit you with panic"
- LOGOS: "I will visit you with panic"
- PNEUMA: "sudden terror, wasting disease, and burning fever" (no separate "visit" verb)
- Ruling: "I will visit you with panic" — LOGOS; "visit with" for *pakad* is the covenant-legal rendering; PNEUMA restructures the clause, eliminating the specific verbal form

**D-033 | V. 16 | "Make the heart ache" vs. "drains your life away"**
- EMET: "making the soul pine away [medevot nefesh]"
- LOGOS: "make the heart ache"
- PNEUMA: "drains your life away"
- Ruling: "make the heart ache" — LOGOS; *medevot nefesh* (causing the soul/heart to languish in grief) is a slow-grief image; "heart ache" captures this; PNEUMA's "drains your life away" suggests physical depletion rather than emotional languishing

**D-034 | V. 16 | "Consume the eyes"**
- EMET: "consuming the eyes [mekhallot einayim]"
- LOGOS: "consume the eyes"
- PNEUMA: "consumes your eyes"
- Ruling: "consume the eyes" — LOGOS/EMET; minor variation only (PNEUMA adds "your" before "eyes"); LOGOS preferred for rhythm

### Verse 18

**D-035 | V. 18 | "Discipline" vs. "punish" for yasar**
- EMET: "I will add to discipline you [le-yassar etkhem] sevenfold"
- LOGOS: "I will discipline you again sevenfold"
- PNEUMA: "I will go on to punish you seven times over"
- Ruling: "I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins" — LOGOS; "discipline" for *yasar* (corrective chastisement) is more precise than "punish" (PNEUMA); "sevenfold" over "seven times over" (PNEUMA) — see note on D-036

**D-036 | V. 18 | "Sevenfold" vs. "seven times over"**
- EMET: "seven-times over [sheva]"
- LOGOS: "sevenfold"
- PNEUMA: "seven times over"
- Ruling: "sevenfold" — LOGOS; compound form is the established English rendering of *sheva* in this formula; applied consistently at vv. 18, 21, 24, 28

### Verse 19

**D-037 | V. 19 | "Pride of your power" vs. "pride you take in your own strength"**
- EMET: "the pride of your strength [ge'on uzekhem]"
- LOGOS: "the pride of your power"
- PNEUMA: "the pride you take in your own strength"
- Ruling: "the pride of your power" — LOGOS; "power" for *oz* and "pride" for *ga'on* are both precise; PNEUMA's expansion ("you take in your own") adds words not in the Hebrew

**D-038 | V. 19 | "Heavens like iron" vs. "sky like iron"**
- EMET: "heavens like iron [shamayim ka-barzel]"
- LOGOS: "your heavens like iron"
- PNEUMA: "your sky like iron"
- Ruling: "your heavens like iron" — LOGOS/EMET; "heavens" is the established rendering of *shamayim* in Leviticus covenant texts; "sky" (PNEUMA) is more colloquial

### Verse 21

**D-039 | V. 21 | "Walk contrary to me" vs. "continue to oppose me"**
- EMET: "you walk contrary to me [be-qeri]"
- LOGOS: "you walk contrary to me"
- PNEUMA: "you continue to oppose me"
- Ruling: "you walk contrary to me" — EMET/LOGOS; *qeri* must be rendered consistently as "contrary" across all seven occurrences in the chapter (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41); PNEUMA varies between "oppose" and "continue to oppose," breaking the chapter's terminological spine

**D-040 | V. 21 | "I will continue striking you" vs. "I will go on to strike you"**
- EMET: "I will add strikes upon you [yosafti lehakot etkhem]"
- LOGOS: "I will continue striking you"
- PNEUMA: "I will go on to strike you seven times harder"
- Ruling: "I will continue striking you sevenfold for your sins" — LOGOS; EMET's "add strikes" is overly wooden; PNEUMA adds "harder" which is not in the MT

### Verse 22

**D-041 | V. 22 | "Let loose the wild beasts" vs. "send wild animals"**
- EMET: "I will send against you the wild beasts of the field [chayat ha-sadeh]"
- LOGOS: "I will let loose the wild beasts against you"
- PNEUMA: "I will send wild animals among you"
- Ruling: "I will let loose the wild beasts against you" — LOGOS; "let loose" for *shilachti* (Piel) conveys unleashing; "beasts" over PNEUMA's "animals" — see ruling above

**D-042 | V. 22 | "Bereave you of your children" vs. "rob you of your children"**
- EMET: "that will bereave you of your children [shikkel etkhem]"
- LOGOS: "shall bereave you of your children"
- PNEUMA: "they will rob you of your children"
- Ruling: "shall bereave you of your children" — LOGOS/EMET; "bereave" is the precise term for parental loss of children (*shikkel*); "rob" (PNEUMA) misses the specific bereavement force

### Verse 23

**D-043 | V. 23 | "By these things you are not turned to me" vs. "after all this discipline"**
- EMET: "by these [things] you will not be corrected [lo tivaseru]"
- LOGOS: "if by these things you are not turned to me"
- PNEUMA: "If after all this discipline you still do not turn back to me"
- Ruling: "if by these things you are not turned to me" — LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "all this discipline" as interpretive expansion; "turned" captures the Niphal of *yasar* (to be disciplined/turned) better than EMET's "corrected"

### Verse 25

**D-044 | V. 25 | "Execute vengeance for the covenant" vs. "vengeance of the covenant"**
- EMET: "executing the vengeance of the covenant [niqmat berit]"
- LOGOS: "that shall execute vengeance for the covenant"
- PNEUMA: "to execute the vengeance of the covenant"
- Ruling: "that shall execute vengeance for the covenant" — LOGOS; relative clause construction is most natural in English; "for the covenant" slightly preferable to "of the covenant" (EMET/PNEUMA) as it preserves the instrumental force — the sword enforces the covenant

**D-045 | V. 25 | "Gather within your cities" vs. "gather in your towns"**
- EMET: "gather into your cities [ve-ne'asaftem el-areikhem]"
- LOGOS: "gather within your cities"
- PNEUMA: "gather in your towns"
- Ruling: "gather within your cities" — LOGOS; *arim* = cities/walled settlements; "within" captures the siege-context (gathering inside the walls); PNEUMA's "towns" is too informal for the siege context

### Verse 26

**D-046 | V. 26 | "Break your supply of bread" vs. "cut off your supply" vs. "staff of bread"**
- EMET: "In my breaking for you the staff of bread [mateh lechem]"
- LOGOS: "When I break your supply of bread"
- PNEUMA: "When I cut off your supply of bread"
- Ruling: "When I break your supply of bread" — LOGOS; "break" for *shavar* is the correct verb (PNEUMA changes it to "cut off"); "supply of bread" is an acceptable interpretation of *mateh lechem* ("staff of bread"); EMET's "staff of bread" is literal but requires a gloss for modern readers

**D-047 | V. 26 | "Dole out your bread again by weight"**
- EMET: "they shall return your bread by weight"
- LOGOS: "shall dole out your bread again by weight"
- PNEUMA: "dole out your bread by weight"
- Ruling: "shall dole out your bread again by weight" — LOGOS; "dole out" conveys rationing; "again" captures *ve-heshivu* ("return/dole back") in the MT; PNEUMA omits "again"

### Verse 28

**D-048 | V. 28 | "Walk contrary to you in fury" vs. "in my burning anger I will oppose you"**
- EMET: "I will walk contrary to you in fury-heat [be-qeri chamat]"
- LOGOS: "I will walk contrary to you in fury"
- PNEUMA: "in my burning anger I will oppose you"
- Ruling: "I will walk contrary to you in fury" — LOGOS; PNEUMA removes the *qeri* ("contrary") dimension entirely, rendering it as pure anger; "walk contrary to" must be preserved for terminological consistency across the chapter; "fury" for *chamat* is precise

**D-049 | V. 28 | "I myself will discipline you"**
- EMET: "even I myself will discipline you sevenfold"
- LOGOS: "I myself will discipline you sevenfold"
- PNEUMA: "punish you seven times over"
- Ruling: "I myself will discipline you sevenfold" — LOGOS; "discipline" vs. "punish" — see D-035; "I myself" for the emphatic pronoun is established in the curse sections

### Verse 29

**D-050 | V. 29 | "The flesh of your sons... the flesh of your daughters" — repetition**
- EMET: "You shall eat the flesh of your sons, and you shall eat the flesh of your daughters"
- LOGOS: "You shall eat the flesh of your sons, and you shall eat the flesh of your daughters"
- PNEUMA: "You will eat the flesh of your own sons and daughters"
- Ruling: LOGOS; MT repeats "you shall eat the flesh of" — the repetition is retained; PNEUMA merges and adds "own" which is not in the MT

### Verse 30

**D-051 | V. 30 | "Dead bodies... dead bodies" vs. "corpses... corpses"**
- EMET: "your corpses upon the corpses of your dung-idols [giluleikhem]"
- LOGOS: "your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols"
- PNEUMA: "your corpses on the corpses of your idols"
- Ruling: "your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols" — LOGOS; "dead bodies" over "corpses" for slightly elevated register; "idols" over EMET's "dung-idols" in the consensus text (the pejorative force of *gilulim* is noted but "idols" is the conventional rendering)

**D-052 | V. 30 | "My soul shall abhor you" — verbal echo with v. 11**
- EMET: "my soul shall abhor you [ve-ga'alah nafshi etkhem]"
- LOGOS: "my soul shall abhor you"
- PNEUMA: "I will turn away from you in disgust"
- Ruling: "my soul shall abhor you" — LOGOS/EMET; the verbal echo with v. 11 ("my soul shall not abhor you") must be preserved; same verb *ga'al*, same object *nafshi*; PNEUMA removes *nafshi* and changes the verb, breaking the echo

### Verse 31

**D-053 | V. 31 | "Lay your cities waste" vs. "demolish your cities"**
- EMET: "I will make your cities a waste [lecharavah]"
- LOGOS: "I will lay your cities waste"
- PNEUMA: "I will demolish your cities"
- Ruling: "I will lay your cities waste" — LOGOS; "lay waste" is the established covenant-curse idiom; "demolish" (PNEUMA) is too physically specific and loses the covenantal resonance

**D-054 | V. 31 | "I will not smell your pleasing aromas"**
- EMET: "I will not smell your aroma of soothing [reiach nikhokheikhem]"
- LOGOS: "I will not smell your pleasing aromas"
- PNEUMA: "I will not inhale the soothing fragrance of your offerings"
- Ruling: "I will not smell your pleasing aromas" — LOGOS; "pleasing aromas" is the established Levitical sacrifice-acceptance formula (*reiach nikhokh*) used throughout the book; PNEUMA adds "offerings" which is not in the MT here; "smell" over PNEUMA's "inhale" — the verb *ariah* is simply "smell/detect scent"

### Verse 33

**D-055 | V. 33 | "Unsheathe the sword after you" vs. "draw out the sword to pursue you"**
- EMET: "I will draw out [hariqoti] the sword behind you"
- LOGOS: "I will unsheathe the sword after you"
- PNEUMA: "draw out the sword to pursue you"
- Ruling: "I will unsheathe the sword after you" — LOGOS; "unsheathe" for *hariq* (Hiphil of *riq*, "to empty/draw out") is vivid; "after you" for *achareikhhem* is more faithful to the MT's imagery of relentless pursuit than PNEUMA's "to pursue you" (which interprets the directional prepositional phrase)

### Verse 36

**D-056 | V. 36 | "Sound of a driven leaf" vs. "sound of a wind-driven leaf"**
- EMET: "the sound of a driven leaf [qol aleh niddaf]"
- LOGOS: "The sound of a driven leaf"
- PNEUMA: "the sound of a wind-driven leaf"
- Ruling: "The sound of a driven leaf" — EMET/LOGOS; MT does not specify wind; "wind-driven" (PNEUMA) adds specificity not in the Hebrew

**D-057 | V. 36 | "Put them to flight" vs. "send them fleeing" vs. "pursue them"**
- EMET: "shall pursue them" (literal for *radaf* = "pursue")
- LOGOS: "shall put them to flight"
- PNEUMA: "will send them fleeing"
- Ruling: "shall put them to flight" — LOGOS; EMET's "pursue them" is literally correct for *radaf* but awkward — a leaf does not literally pursue; "put them to flight" better conveys the idiomatic meaning; PNEUMA's "send them fleeing" is very close and acceptable but "put to flight" is more formal

### Verse 39

**D-058 | V. 39 | "Rot away" vs. "waste away" vs. "melt away"**
- EMET: "shall melt away [yimmaqqu] in their iniquity"
- LOGOS: "shall rot away in your enemies' lands because of their iniquity"
- PNEUMA: "will waste away in the lands of their enemies because of their own iniquities"
- Ruling: "shall rot away in your enemies' lands because of their iniquity" — LOGOS; *maqaq* (Niphal) denotes slow organic dissolution; "rot away" captures this better than "waste away" (PNEUMA, more general) or "melt away" (EMET, suggests liquid dissolution)

**D-059 | V. 39 | "Their fathers" vs. "their ancestors"**
- EMET: "the iniquities of their fathers [avotam]"
- LOGOS: "the iniquities of their fathers"
- PNEUMA: "their ancestors' iniquities"
- Ruling: "the iniquities of their fathers" — EMET/LOGOS; *avot* = "fathers" in the covenant-genealogical sense; "ancestors" (PNEUMA) is accurate but less specific to the father-son covenantal succession the text emphasizes

### Verse 40

**D-060 | V. 40 | "Treachery" for ma'al**
- EMET: "their act of treachery [ma'alam]"
- LOGOS: "their treachery that they committed against me"
- PNEUMA: "their treachery against me"
- Ruling: "their treachery that they committed against me" — LOGOS; "committed against me" preserves the explicit divine-object clause of the MT; PNEUMA drops "that they committed"

### Verse 41

**D-061 | V. 41 | "Uncircumcised heart" — singular vs. plural**
- EMET: "their uncircumcised heart [levavam he'arel]" (singular)
- LOGOS: "their uncircumcised heart" (singular)
- PNEUMA: "their uncircumcised hearts" (plural)
- Ruling: "their uncircumcised heart" (singular) — EMET/LOGOS; MT has singular collective *levavam*; the collective singular preserves the unified community dimension of the repentance; "hearts" (PNEUMA) is grammatically natural in English but deviates from the Hebrew

### Verse 44

**D-062 | V. 44 | "Spurn them... abhor them" vs. "reject them... turn away from"**
- EMET: "I will not reject them [lo ma'astim] and I will not abhor them [lo ge'altim]"
- LOGOS: "I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them"
- PNEUMA: "I will not reject them or turn away from them"
- Ruling: "I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them" — LOGOS; "spurn" creates echo with v. 15 ("if you spurn my statutes") — same verb *ma'as* used for Israel's rejection of the statutes now applied negatively to God's refusal to reject Israel; "abhor" for *ga'al* creates triple echo across vv. 11, 30, 44 — the theological arc of the chapter (soul shall not abhor → soul shall abhor → I will not abhor) is crucial and must be preserved; PNEUMA's "turn away from them" for *ga'al* breaks both echoes

---

## Structural / Terminology Rulings

**T-001 | qeri rendered throughout as "contrary."** The term *qeri* (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41) is rendered "contrary" in all occurrences: "walk contrary to me" (Israel), "walk contrary to you" (YHWH), "walking contrary to me" (confession). PNEUMA's varied renderings ("oppose," "opposition") are overridden in every instance for terminological consistency.

**T-002 | Sevenfold formula rendered "sevenfold" throughout.** "Sevenfold" (vv. 18, 21, 24, 28) is used uniformly; PNEUMA's "seven times over" and EMET's "seven-times over" are both overridden. The compound form preserves the ritual-number force of *sheva*.

**T-003 | ga'al verbal echo preserved across vv. 11, 30, 44.** The root *ga'al* ("abhor") appears at v. 11 ("my soul shall not abhor you" — the blessing), v. 30 ("my soul shall abhor you" — the curse), and v. 44 ("neither will I abhor them" — the grace-floor). "Abhor" is used consistently for all three occurrences to preserve this deliberate theological arc.

**T-004 | ma'as verbal echo preserved across vv. 15 and 44.** *Ma'as* ("to spurn/reject") appears at v. 15 (Israel spurns God's statutes) and v. 44 (God will not spurn Israel). "Spurn" is used for both to preserve the covenantal irony: Israel does to God's statutes precisely what God refuses to do to Israel.

**T-005 | "Pleasing aromas" retained as fixed sacrificial formula.** The term *reiach nikhokh* at v. 31 is rendered "pleasing aromas" — consistent with all prior uses in Leviticus — so that the reversal ("I will not smell") registers against the expected background of sacrifice acceptance.
